subj-group.subject: THE JOURNAL OF GERONTOLOGY: Medical Sciences

title-group.article-title: Weighted vest use or resistance exercise to offset muscle loss in older adults: secondary findings from the INVEST in bone health RCT

contrib.aff: Department of Biomedical Engineering, Wake Forest University School of Medicine, Winston-Salem, North Carolina, United States

author-notes.corresp: Address correspondence to: Ashley A. Weaver, PhD. E-mail: ashley.weaver@wfusm.edu

license.license-p: This is an Open Access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-NoDerivs licence (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/), which permits non-commercial reproduction and distribution of the work, in any medium, provided the original work is not altered or transformed in any way, and that the work is properly cited. For commercial re-use, please contact reprints@oup.com for reprints and translation rights for reprints. All other permissions can be obtained through our RightsLink service via the Permissions link on the article page on our site—for further information please contact journals.permissions@oup.com.

sec.title: Background
sec.p: The INVEST in Bone Health Trial examined the effects of weight loss (WL), WL plus resistance training (WL + RT), or WL plus weighted vest use (WL + VEST) on musculoskeletal health. This secondary analysis evaluated changes in muscle area and density using computed tomography (CT) and lean and fat mass using dual-energy x-ray absorptiometry (DXA).

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: One hundred fifty participants (50/group) were randomized to 12-months of WL, WL + RT, or WL + VEST, undergoing CT and DXA scans at baseline, six- and 12-months. DXA measured lean and fat mass, while CT assessed muscle and intermuscular adipose tissue (IMAT) cross-sectional area (CSA) and density. Mixed linear models evaluated changes and treatment effects, and partial Pearson’s correlations examined relationships between weight change and CT/DXA outcomes.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Participants (66.4 ± 4.6 years, 75% female, 69% white) were living with overweight (14.7%) or obesity (85.3%). All groups achieved similar and significant weight loss (∼10%). At 12-months, WL + RT increased mid-thigh muscle CSA (0.5%, p < .05), improved muscle density (3.7%-5.9%, p < .03), and reduced IMAT (20%-22%, p < .05) and fat masses (22%-26.8%, all p < .061). At the trunk, WL + VEST showed a trend toward muscle preservation and improved density (4.2%, p = .08) compared to WL, but had minimal impact on other measures. Differences between WL + VEST and WL were insignificant (all p > .05), but group comparisons showed improvements for WL + RT. Weight loss correlated with increased muscle density (r < 0, p < .001) but reduced muscle CSA and IMAT (r > 0, p < .001), indicating improved quality but reduced quantity.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: Our findings underscore the significance of weight loss-associated muscle loss, highlighting progressive RT as a minimally effective preservation strategy.

institution-wrap.institution: National Center for Advancing Translational Sciences (NCATS) and National Institutes of Health

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Among older adults undergoing weight loss (WL), the associated loss of fat-free mass varies widely, accounting for 4%-38% of total WL depending on the study and the included measure of fat-free mass.1 This variability highlights the potential for substantial reductions in fat-free mass, which approximates lean mass, during WL. Although the majority of weight lost is typically fat mass—often associated with clinically meaningful improvements in metabolic health and physical function, such as enhanced glycemic control and reduced cardiovascular risk—this concurrent loss of lean mass may contribute to declines in muscle strength, increased frailty, and reduced functional capacity.2 Thus, it is important to strike a balance between loss of fat mass and loss of fat-free mass to achieve optimal functional outcomes, particularly for older adults undergoing intentional WL who are more susceptible to muscle wasting and low muscle mass.3–5 As such, WL strategies for this population should ideally be designed to minimize lean mass loss while still eliciting meaningful fat mass reductions.6

Dietary interventions and exercise are just a few of the potential strategies to be considered for safe and effective WL in older adults. Although caloric restriction effectively reduces body weight by approximately 10%, it often causes a loss of fat-free mass, which may be mitigated by adjusting the nutritional composition of the diet. For example, diets using high-protein meal replacements have been shown to result in fat and body weight losses while maintaining lean mass in older adults.7–9 Thus, it appears higher protein intake can help protect musculoskeletal health during WL for this population.10,11 Alongside diet, exercise can be employed to further support this goal. Progressive loading exercises, such as resistance training (RT), have consistently demonstrated an ability to preserve and even increase skeletal muscle mass during WL.12,13 However, older adults face challenges with RT exercise adherence due to factors such as limited accessibility, musculoskeletal pain, or lack of social support.14 As an alternative, weighted vests offer a more accessible method for applying progressive loading which may provide similar benefits without the need for traditional RT.15–18 Although weighted vests have proven feasible for use in older adults,16 it remains unclear whether this interventional strategy, when combined with dietary WL, can preserve lean mass loss and benefit overall muscle health.

WL trials have traditionally used dual-energy x-ray absorptiometry (DXA) to estimate muscle mass, but DXA approximates whole-body lean mass,19 including water, connective tissue, skin, and organs, limiting its accuracy.20 Despite these shortcomings, DXA remains widely used to assess fat-free mass changes in research and in clinical practice. In contrast, computed tomography (CT) offers a more precise and detailed evaluation of muscle, capable of directly measuring both muscle mass and quality. The cross-sectional area of skeletal muscle at the third lumbar (L3) vertebra on abdominal CT strongly correlates with total body muscle mass21 and is a key indicator for sarcopenia diagnosis.21 Paraspinous muscle areas at the twelfth thoracic (T12) vertebra, measured on chest or abdominal CT, are clinically relevant due to their role in spinal support and associations with back pain and mobility.22 Beyond spinal musculature, lower extremity muscle is also crucial, as thigh muscle assessments in aging populations have been used to detect intervention effects earlier than lumbar measurements.23

Previously, obtaining these muscle measurements at multiple levels on CT was a highly manual and time-consuming process, limiting comprehensive assessments. As a result, most studies have focused on single anatomical sites rather than the full extent of musculoskeletal changes at various regions. However, advancements in automated CT analysis now allow for a streamlined and standardized process of muscle quantification, improving feasibility and clinical applicability.24,25 Given its superior sensitivity, CT overcomes the limitations of DXA by providing detailed assessments of muscle changes, making CT a powerful supplemental tool for evaluating musculoskeletal adaptations to WL interventions.

The INVEST in Bone Health randomized clinical trial (NCT04076618) was designed to test the hypothesis that the addition of weighted vests to WL (WL + VEST) would better preserve bone health compared to WL alone (WL), and similarly to WL combined with a structured resistance training (WL + RT) intervention, among older adults living with overweight or obesity.26 While the primary focus of the study was on bone health,27 this secondary analysis will explore the effects of WL, WL + VEST, or WL + RT on muscle health, specifically looking at changes in fat-free mass and lean mass. This analysis leverages both CT and DXA imaging to examine the longitudinal effects of WL interventions on regional muscle, fat and lean mass changes. We hypothesize that both RT and weighted vest use, as progressive loading interventions, will enhance muscle and lean mass compared to WL alone, with site-specific effects—vest use benefiting the trunk more than other regions. Additionally, we expect WL to correlate strongly with changes in muscle, fat, and lean mass across all groups.

sec.title: Study design and oversight
sec.p: The INVEST in Bone Health trial (NCT04076618) was conducted from September 2019 through April 2024 at Wake Forest University and Atrium Health Wake Forest Baptist Medical Center in Winston-Salem, NC. Details of the study design and methods along with primary outcome results have been previously published.26–28 The INVEST in Bone Health trial was approved by the Wake Forest University School of Medicine Institutional Review Board (IRB No.00058279) and is registered on ClinicalTrials.gov (NCT04076618), where primary study results are publicly accessible.

sec.title: Participants
sec.p: Participants were recruited through advertisements and targeted mailings. A thorough screening process was used to determine eligibility based on the following inclusion criteria: (1) 60-85 years of age, (2) BMI 30-40 kg/m2 or 27.0 ≤ 30.0 kg/m2 plus one obesity-related risk factor/clinical comorbidity, (3) <5% weight loss in the past 6 months, (4) no self-disclosed contraindications for participation in exercise training/vest use, and (5) willing to provide informed consent and agree to all study procedures/assessments. Those with severe cardiometabolic disease (e.g., recent myocardial infarction, unstable angina, uncontrolled hypertension, or diabetes) or musculoskeletal (e.g., osteoporosis, severe arthritis/back pain) impairments that precluded weighted vest use/exercise training, cognitive impairment, or who used prescription osteoporosis or WL medications within the past year were excluded. Further details on participant recruitment, eligibility, and enrollment can be found elsewhere.26–28 Out of 442 participants assessed for eligibility, 150 met all inclusion criteria and were enrolled in the study.

sec.title: Interventions
sec.p: Once enrolled, the 150 individuals were randomized via a web-based randomization system, stratified by sex with random block sizes (3, 6, 9, and 12) to one of the three study interventions (n = 50/group): WL, WL + VEST, or WL + RT. After randomization, the participants completed 12 continuous months of their assigned intervention.

The INVEST in Bone Health interventions have been previously described in detail.26,29 Briefly, all participants received the same WL program which combined a hypocaloric, nutritionally complete, partial meal replacement program (Medifast, Inc.) with group nutrition classes designed to elicit ∼10% body WL over the 12-month interventional period. Participants in the WL + RT group also completed a supervised, progressive RT intervention occurring three nonconsecutive days per week and consisting of three sets of 10-12 repetitions for eight different upper and lower body exercises at 70%-75% 1 repetition max (1RM). Those in the WL + VEST group wore a weighted vest daily, gradually increasing to a target of 8 h per day during their most active periods. Initially, the vest was unloaded (i.e., one-pound vest) and the weight was increased weekly (using 1/8th pound blocks) to match participant’s WL progress. The vest was loaded up to 10% of the participant’s baseline body weight. Daily logs were used to monitor vest weight and wear time.

sec.title: Computed tomography
sec.p: At baseline, six- and 12-month timepoints, participants received a helical CT scan obtained on a 64-slice PET/CT GE Discovery (Medical Systems, Madison WI) scanner with a standard abdomen reconstruction filter at 2.5-mm slice thickness. Scan coverage spanned from the mid-chest to approximately 3-cm below mid-shaft of the femurs. Scans were acquired with 50-cm field of view, 120 kV, 1:1 pitch, and auto-exposure.

From the CT scans, single slice DICOM images were extracted for three anatomical regions of interest: (1) mid-thigh (defined as half the length between the superior aspect of the greater trochanter and the inferior aspect of the lateral condyle), (2) trunk (defined at the midpoint of the L3 vertebrae), and (3) paraspinous (defined at the pedicle of the T12 vertebrae). To select these DICOM slices, previously trained machine learning models were employed using the U-Net methodology described in the appendix of Lenchik et al. (2021) to automatically extract a single slice corresponding to each of our three regions of interest.25 These models were trained with manually selected slices as ground truth data.

The extracted DICOM slices were then fed into the second layer of our group’s machine learning, image-processing pipeline designed to segment skeletal muscle (−29 to 150 HUs) and intermuscular adipose tissue (IMAT; −190 to −30 HUs). Tissue at the paraspinous and trunk levels was segmented with a previously described machine learning methodology again employing the U-Net network architecture.24,25 At the mid-thigh level, we used a two-model approach to preserve the ability to separately analyze tissue in the left thigh versus the right thigh. First, a DeepLabV3+ model,30 trained using a random selection of manually annotated mid-thigh slices, segmented each slice image into three classes: left thigh, right thigh, and background. Next, the left and right thigh segmentations were separately used to mask the anatomical image, which were then segmented by a U-Net model like those used for the paraspinous and trunk levels. When needed, manual corrections to the resulting segmentations were performed by an experienced reader (SDL). Skeletal muscle and IMAT cross-sectional areas (CSA; in cm2) and densities (in HU) were extracted for the mid-thigh (left and right, individually) and trunk. Given the bilateral symmetry of paraspinous muscles, skeletal muscle CSA (in cm2) and density (in HU) were measured on the left side only, as this unilateral measure is an established predictor of all-cause mortality.25 At the T12 paraspinous level, IMAT was not assessed as it has proven to have weaker associations with functional outcomes and greater measurement variability compared to other regions.31 In contrast, the trunk (at L3) and mid-thigh regions have shown to exhibit higher fatty infiltration, making these more clinically relevant sites for IMAT assessment.32

sec.title: Dual-energy x-ray absorptiometry
sec.p: In addition to CT scans, participants underwent whole-body, hip and spine DXA scanning with a GE iDXA (Medical Systems, Madison, WI) at baseline, six- and 12-month timepoints. These scans measured lean and fat masses (in kg) for the trunk and legs regions to align with CT-derived measures. All scans followed manufacturer-recommended positioning guidelines and were reviewed by a DXA technologist certified by the International Society of Clinical Densitometry.

sec.title: Statistical analyses
sec.p: Power for the INVEST in Bone Health trial was determined based on group differences in the primary outcomes of volumetric and areal hip bone mineral density (BMD). All analyses in this manuscript were secondary and should be considered hypothesis-generating. Baseline descriptive statistics were used to summarize demographic and clinical characteristics overall and by intervention group. To assess longitudinal changes and treatment effects, only participants who received a baseline scan and at least one follow-up scan (six- or 12-months) were included in the respective analyses. Outcomes were analyzed using mixed effects linear models adjusted for treatment effect, visit (6 or 12 months), treatment by visit interaction, sex, thigh side (right/left, where applicable to account for repeated measures), and baseline value of specified outcome as fixed effects, and participant IDs as random intercepts. Outcomes are presented as the change in mean (95% CI) and percent change from baseline for each timepoint. Additionally, contrast statements were utilized with these models to make pairwise comparisons between treatment groups (i.e., WL + VEST vs WL + RT), testing for differences between groups at each timepoint.

Partial Pearson’s correlations, adjusted for sex, were used to assess the relationship between change in body weight and change in all CT/DXA outcomes, assuming normally distributed changes. For all variables of interest, correlations were conducted separately for six-month and 12-month changes, comparing six-month body weight change with six-month outcome changes and 12-month body weight change with 12-month outcome changes. In all analyses, statistical significance was determined as a p-value < .05.

sec.title: Participant characteristics and intervention process measures
sec.p: The study enrolled 150 older adults (66.4 ± 4.6 years), with 133 (89%) completing the entire 12-month intervention. Specifically, 43 participants completed the study in the WL alone group (n = 3 lost to follow up, n = 4 refused further contact), 45 completed in the WL + VEST group (n = 2 lost to follow up, n = 3 refused further contact), and 45 completed in the WL + VEST group (n = 1 lost to follow up, n = 4 refused further contact). Details on the study cohort have been previously published,29 and details on demographics and clinical characteristics are provided in the online Supplementary Materials (Table S1). Participants were predominantly female (75%), White/Caucasian (69%), and living with overweight (14.7%; 25 ≤ BMI < 30 kg/m2) or obesity (85.3%; BMI ≥ 30 kg/m2).

All intervention groups achieved significant and comparable WL ranging from 9.7%-11.2%, with the majority of weight lost by six-months and maintained through 12-months. Mean weight changes were similar across groups: WL Δ: −8.9 kg (95% CI: −10.6, −7.3) at six-months and −8.9 kg (95% CI: −10.7, −7.0) at 12-months; WL + VEST Δ: −8.7 kg (95% CI: −10.3, −7.0) at six-months and −8.4 kg (95% CI: −10.2, −6.5) at 12-months; and WL + RT Δ: −9.6 kg (95% CI: −11.3, −8.0) at six-months and −10.4 kg (95% CI: −12.3, −8.6) at 12-months. Adherence varied by intervention with WL + VEST participants wearing the vests for an average of 7.1 ± 1.5 h/day (target was 8 h/day) and 81.8% ± 22.8% of lost body weight was replaced in the vest. Because the intervention was designed to target 10% weight loss, a participant with baseline weight of 200 lb would aim to lose 20 lb; in this example, 81.8% of that loss (∼16 lb) would be added to the weighted vest. WL + RT participants attended 71.3% ± 19.2% of classes, averaging 2.1 ± 0.6 sessions/week over 12-months (target was three sessions/week).

sec.title: Baseline data and longitudinal changes
sec.p: Table 1 presents baseline aggregate mean and standard deviations for each CT and DXA outcome along with six- and 12-month longitudinal changes in these outcomes by intervention groups. Of the total 150 participants, available sample sizes for analysis varied by measure: 125 (83%) for T12 paraspinous, 132 (88%) for mid-thigh, and 140 (93%) for trunk measures and all DXA outcomes. Inclusion in the present analysis required a baseline and at least one follow-up scan (six- or 12-month), the region of interest within the field of view, and no metal or artifacts interfering with measurements.

caption.p: Longitudinal changes in CT and DXA muscle and fat outcomes by treatment group.

fn.p: Abbreviations: CSA, cross-sectional area; HU, Hounsfield units; IMAT, intermuscular adipose tissue; RT, resistance training; WL, weight loss.

fn.label: a
fn.p: N represents the number of participants with a baseline measure and at least one follow-up measure (6 or 12 months) who are included in the analyses for each outcome.

Note: Mixed effects linear models use treatment effect, visit, treatment by visit interaction, sex, thigh side (where applicable to account for repeated left/right measuresb), and baseline values of each outcome as fixed effects and participant IDs as random effects.

sec.p: The statistical model accounts for repeated measures (i.e., right and left sides) so mid-thigh CT outcomes effectively represent the average of both sides, allowing us to incorporate information from both limbs into the mid-thigh estimates discussed below. Longitudinally, consistent patterns were observed across all groups and regions as shown in Figure 1. At both six- and 12-month timepoints, IMAT CSA decreased substantially (13.3%-22.7%) while muscle density increased (1.2%-5.9%). Additionally, muscle CSA decreased in all groups except for the WL + RT group, where mid-thigh muscle CSA slightly increased by 0.5% at 12-months.

caption.p: Six- and 12-month changes by treatment group for CT-derived muscle cross-sectional area (CSA in cm2) and muscle density (in HU) measures at the paraspinous, trunk, and mid-thigh regions. Significant group differences are denoted as follows: * for WL + RT versus WL, ** for WL + VEST versus WL + RT, and *** for both WL + RT versus WL and WL + VEST versus WL + RT (made with GraphPad prism and BioRender).

sec.p: Six- and 12-month changes by group can be seen in Figure 2. Overall, trunk lean mass was the only outcome that increased from baseline for all groups (0.9%-1.9%), with highest increases among the WL + RT and WL + VEST groups. All groups experienced significant reductions in fat mass for the trunk (all >20%) and legs (14.1%-22.1%) regions, with the greatest percent decreases in the WL + RT group for all regions.

caption.p: Six- and 12-month changes by treatment group for DXA-derived lean mass (in kg) and fat mass (in kg) for the following regions: trunk and legs only. Significant group differences are denoted as follows: * for WL + RT versus WL, ** for WL + VEST versus WL + RT, and *** for both WL + RT versus WL and WL + VEST versus WL + RT (made with GraphPad prism and BioRender).

sec.title: Treatment effects and group differences
sec.p: Table 2 provides pairwise comparisons evaluating treatment effects for six- and 12-month change in outcomes between groups as follows: WL + VEST versus WL, WL + RT versus WL, and WL + VEST versus WL + RT.

caption.p: Between-group treatment effects on CT and DXA muscle and fat outcomesa.

tr.th: WL + VEST vs WL Δ Mean (95% CI), p value
WL + RT vs WL Δ Mean (95% CI), p value
WL + VEST vs WL + RT Δ Mean (95% CI), p value

fn.p: Abbreviations: CSA, cross-sectional area; HU, Hounsfield units; RT, resistance training; IMAT, intermuscular adipose tissue; WL, weight loss.

fn.label: a
fn.p: Statistically significant outcomes, defined as p < .05, are highlighted in bold.

fn.label: b
fn.p: N represents the number of participants with a baseline measure and at least one follow-up measure (6 or 12 months) who are included in the analyses for each outcome.

Note: Mixed effects linear models use treatment effect, visit, treatment by visit interaction, sex, thigh side (where applicable to account for repeated left/right measuresc), and baseline values of each outcome as fixed effects and participant IDs as random effects.

sec.p: Group comparisons of CT measures showed no significant differences between the WL + VEST and WL alone groups (all p > .05). However, significant differences were identified in the other two comparisons (WL + RT vs WL and WL + VEST vs WL + RT). Specifically, the WL + RT group exhibited greater positive changes (i.e., increases) in paraspinous muscle density (6Mo: p = .047), mid-thigh muscle CSA (12Mo: p = .002), and mid-thigh muscle density (6Mo: p = .002; 12Mo: p = .029) compared to the WL alone group. Similarly, when compared to WL + VEST group, WL + RT showed greater positive changes (i.e., increases) in paraspinous muscle density (12Mo: p = .010) and mid-thigh muscle density (6Mo: p = .007; 12Mo: p = .013). For IMAT measures, WL + RT demonstrated greater negative changes (i.e., decreases) in mid-thigh IMAT CSA at both six- and 12-month timepoints compared to WL alone (both p < .05). Additionally, WL + RT showed smaller negative changes (i.e., decreases) in trunk muscle CSA compared to WL alone (6Mo: p = .003).

For DXA outcomes, there were again no significant differences observed in six- or 12-month comparisons between the WL + VEST and WL alone groups (all p > .05). However, significant differences were noted in other group comparisons. Specifically, the WL + RT group showed greater negative changes (i.e., decreases) in legs fat mass (12Mo: both p < .004) compared to both the WL alone and WL + VEST groups. When compared to the WL + VEST group only, WL + RT demonstrated greater negative changes (i.e., decreases) in trunk fat mass (12Mo: p = .030) and legs lean mass (12Mo: p = .035). Across all three groups, no significant differences were found for changes in trunk lean masses (all p > .05).

sec.title: Correlation of CT/DXA changes with body weight changes
sec.p: Table 3 shows partial Pearson’s correlations of six- and 12-month changes in CT/DXA outcomes with corresponding changes in body weight for each timepoint.

caption.p: Partial Pearson’s correlations between change in body weight and change in CT/DXA muscle and fat outcomes, adjusted for sexa.

fn.p: Abbreviations: CSA, cross-sectional area; IMAT, intermuscular adipose tissue.

fn.label: a
fn.p: Statistically significant correlations, defined as p < .05, are highlighted in bold.

fn.label: b
fn.p: The corresponding timepoint is used for body weight comparisons with outcomes (e.g., six-month weight loss is correlated with six-month changes in outcomes).

sec.p: For CT measures, changes in muscle and fat outcomes at six- and 12-months were significantly correlated with the corresponding six- and 12-month changes in body weight (all p < .05). Across all timepoints, body weight changes were positively correlated with changes in muscle and IMAT CSAs (all r > 0), indicating that greater WL was associated with greater reductions in muscle and IMAT. In contrast, body weight changes were negatively correlated with changes in muscle densities (all r < 0), highlighting that WL was linked to improvements in muscle density across all regions.

For DXA measures, changes in lean and fat masses at six- and 12-months were also found to significantly correlate with corresponding six- and 12-month changes in body weight (all p < .044). For both timepoints, changes in body weight were positively correlated with changes in trunk fat mass as well as legs lean and fat masses (all r > 0). This result suggests loss of body weight was associated with greater loss of fat across all regions and reduced lean mass in the extremities. However, lean mass in the trunk region was negatively correlated (all r < 0) with changes in body weight, suggesting that lost weight was associated with increased lean mass for that region.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: The purpose of this secondary analysis of the INVEST in Bone Health trial was to examine and compare the longitudinal effects of WL, WL + RT, and WL + VEST on imaging-derived measures of muscle, fat and lean mass among older adults living with overweight or obesity who were actively undergoing WL. We hypothesized that RT and weighted vest use, when combined with WL, would enhance muscle and lean mass more compared to WL alone. Our findings partially supported this hypothesis as WL + RT effectively preserved muscle and lean mass, improved muscle density (a marker of quality), and significantly reduced fat tissue compared to the other groups. While WL + VEST was expected to yield similar outcomes to WL + RT on muscle preservation, we observed the effect of weighted vest use on muscle outcomes to be largely nonsignificant and comparable to the WL alone group. Notably, this study leveraged both CT and DXA assessments, highlighting the strengths of each in tracking musculoskeletal changes and demonstrating the utility of machine learning for efficient, longitudinal CT analysis.

The extent to which RT can help attenuate WL-associated declines in muscle was explored in a 2018 meta-analysis of six randomized controlled trials (RCTs) that compared the effects of caloric restriction with and without RT in older adults living with obesity. In this analysis, despite similar reductions in body mass and fat mass across all groups, the addition of RT resulted in 93.5% less loss of lean body mass compared to caloric restriction alone.33 These findings, supported by other studies, highlight the effectiveness of RT as a strategy to offset lean mass loss during WL. Our study further supports these results as we consistently found WL + RT to be the most effective interventional strategy with regard to our muscle and fat measures. Specifically, our CT analyses showed that WL + RT minimized muscle loss in the abdominal and mid-thigh regions, even resulting in a net gain of muscle at the mid-thigh cross-section by 0.5% by the 12-month timepoint. WL + RT also led to greater improvements in muscle density and substantial reductions in IMAT, indicating that muscle quality was improved over the interventional period. DXA results—although influenced by a higher degree of variability—showed a similar trend of decreased muscle quantity and improved quality for the WL + RT group, with fat mass losses across all regions significant and comparable to reductions in IMAT as measured by CT. Our interventional group comparisons consistently revealed notable differences in the effects on muscle, lean, and fat masses/quality between the WL + RT group and the other two groups. These findings further support the existing literature, emphasizing that RT is a highly effective strategy for targeting fat loss and preservation of muscle during WL for older adults living with overweight or obesity.

Despite the clear benefits of RT, it remains inaccessible for many older adults and exercise compliance is a major barrier. This challenge inspired the INVEST in Bone Health trial’s central question: Can a weighted vest provide similar musculoskeletal benefits to traditional RT while improving outcomes beyond WL alone? In this secondary analysis focused on muscle and lean mass, we did observe that WL + VEST attenuated muscle losses more than WL alone at the trunk (−2.8% vs −4.9% at 12-months), although WL + RT preserved the most overall (−1.8% at 12-months). For the paraspinous region, WL + VEST was most effective at minimizing 12-month muscle loss (−4.1%), followed by WL + RT (−6.5%) and WL alone (−7.8%). Compared to WL alone, the WL + VEST group also experienced enhanced muscle density at the trunk (+2.7%) with results similar to the WL + RT group (+3.0%). Interestingly, when focusing on the DXA outcomes, the WL + VEST group showed the least lean mass loss in the legs—an unexpected finding given that the vest’s progressive loading does not directly target these areas. Cumulatively, these results suggest that the WL + VEST intervention did have minor positive impacts on muscle, lean, and fat mass, offering some benefits over WL alone but remaining less impactful and more nonsignificant compared to our WL + RT intervention.

Overall, our findings do not suggest that weighted vest use provides comparable benefits to traditional resistance training (RT) for preserving muscle. However, it should be noted that the primary outcomes of the INVEST in Bone Health trial also reported no significant group differences across many musculoskeletal measures, including CT- and DXA-derived bone health outcomes.27 Specifically, neither weighted vest use nor progressive RT prevented weight loss—associated bone losses at the hip, as all groups experienced similar and significant declines. The authors suggested this observed treatment heterogeneity might be attributed to possible differences in exercise duration and intensity. In this study, we targeted a 10% weight loss, and up to 10% of that lost weight was subsequently added to the vest. This loading was utilized in the pilot study, which demonstrated signals of increased bone formation and attenuated hip aBMD loss in weighted vest participants, and therefore, informed the design of the broader INVEST in Bone Health trial.15–18 However, to our knowledge, there is no definitive literature establishing the optimal dosing of resistance training or weighted vest loading needed to preserve bone or mitigate bone loss during weight loss in older adults. As a result, participants may have been under-dosed, potentially contributing to the similar, nonsignificant group differences observed in our muscle outcomes in the present study.

Additionally, the primary INVEST in Bone Health study paper emphasized that all participants received a standardized dietary prescription with adequate protein, vitamin D, and calcium intake, which appeared to spare lean mass across groups.27 If lean mass losses were smaller than typically observed in weight loss studies and similar between groups, the absence of significant differences between the WL + VEST and WL groups in our study may be attributable to this dietary intervention and/or insufficient vest loading—an area that should be prioritized in future research to better determine the loading threshold necessary for meaningful muscle preservation. Despite our findings, weighted vest use may still represent a practical strategy for older adults undergoing weight loss, particularly for individuals without access to RT or those at higher risk of frailty, where preserving trunk stability and muscle function is critical to maintaining physical performance. The effects of these interventions on functional outcomes and their relationships with image-based musculoskeletal measures remain to be examined and will be an important focus of future analyses within the INVEST in Bone Health trial.

Beyond intervention-based analyses, we explored correlations between WL and changes in musculoskeletal outcomes (derived from CT and DXA) at six- and 12-months. Notably, WL was associated with improvements in muscle quality (i.e., increased density and decreased IMAT CSA) and simultaneous reductions in muscle quantity (i.e., decreased muscle CSA). Previous studies have shown that individuals living with obesity, despite having higher muscle mass than those of normal weight, often exhibit poorer muscle quality.34 Therefore, it is not a surprising finding that losing excess weight leads to improved muscle quality, as evidenced by reduced fat infiltration and increased density in the INVEST in Bone Health population who lost ∼10% of their body weight over the 12-month interventional period.

This study has several notable strengths. First, the INVEST in Bone Health trial employed a randomized, controlled design with a novel intervention and achieved excellent adherence to the protocol.27 Second, it incorporated advanced assessments of musculoskeletal health, using both CT and DXA imaging to evaluate body composition—an uncommon combination in most WL trials. While DXA is widely used in clinical practice and provides reliable estimates of fat mass, it does not directly measure muscle mass, relying instead on lean mass as a proxy.35 Interestingly, our findings revealed discrepancies between changes in lean mass (by DXA) and muscle mass (by CT). For example, trunk lean mass by DXA increased at six- and 12-month time points across all groups, whereas paraspinous and trunk muscle CSAs decreased during the same periods. Given that muscle loss is expected with WL, we attribute this inconsistency to the limitations of DXA’s lean mass approximation in the trunk region, where water, connective tissue, skin, and organs are included alongside muscle, potentially masking or skewing our results.20 This limitation may be particularly relevant to our study, as hydration changes during WL likely impact abdominal organs, altering trunk lean mass measurements and potentially compromising the reliability of DXA-based muscle assessments in this region. As a result, we feel that CT measures offer a more accurate assessment of muscle in WL trials by directly measuring muscle area and density, particularly in the trunk region.

Historically, a major challenge in using CT for studies like these was the labor-intensive manual effort required to identify regions of interest (e.g., mid-thigh, mid-L3 vertebral level) and segment muscle and fat boundaries for measurement extraction. However, our development and application of machine learning tools24,25 has streamlined this process, enabling efficient analysis of muscle and IMAT on CT scans and making these metrics far more accessible for future research. In an internal validation study, muscle segmentations generated fully automatically by our machine learning algorithms demonstrated excellent agreement with manual segmentations (all p < .001; all r > 0.991) and were approximately ten times faster to obtain, allowing for rapid and accurate measurements across multiple regions of interest. This machine learning-based approach holds substantial promise for opportunistic use of the over 90 million clinical CT scans acquired annually,36 enabling automatic extraction of muscle and fat metrics—similar to those from DXA—which could aid in monitoring age- and WL-related musculoskeletal changes and guiding targeted interventions.

Despite its strengths, the present study has some important limitations to acknowledge. First, muscle measurements were areal (2D) rather than volumetric (3D), which may have limited our ability to fully capture intervention effects, as a single CT slice may not reflect overall muscle volume. However, prior research shows strong correlations between 2D and 3D assessments, supporting the validity of 2D measures for tracking muscle change.37 However, a key challenge in using 2D slices longitudinally is ensuring consistent selection of the same anatomical region across timepoints for appropriate comparisons. At the trunk and paraspinous levels, vertebral landmarks (L3 and T12) allowed our slice selection algorithm to reliably and consistently capture the same region. In contrast, the mid-thigh lacks such landmarks, introducing a higher degree of slice selection variability. To address this, we conducted an internal validation showing that a 10 mm deviation from the true mid-thigh slice produced a maximum measurement error of 2.8%, corresponding to a 4 cm2 muscle area difference in a poor performance case using our machine learning pipelines. This level of error is comparable to intra-user variability reported in other studies using manual segmentation, suggesting that our automated approach likely matches or exceeds manual performance, supporting our view that this represents a minor limitation of the present study.38 Future studies may overcome this limitation by selecting thigh regions nearer to bony landmarks—like the ischial tuberosity—to enhance consistency in longitudinal muscle measurements of the lower extremities.38

Additionally, it is important to note that the study population was predominantly female and geographically constrained to individuals living near the academic center, which may limit the generalizability of our findings. Despite these limitations, we believe our findings for this secondary analysis provide valuable insights that extend beyond the primary objectives of the INVEST in Bone Health trial.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: In conclusion, this secondary analysis of the INVEST in Bone Health trial underscores the critical role of identifying and optimizing methods to counter the negative impacts of WL on muscle health. Of our three interventions, progressive RT was most effective at preserving muscle compared to WL + VEST and WL alone. While less effective than RT and comparable to WL alone, weighted vest use appeared to offer a modest improvement on trunk muscle health outcomes, supporting its potential use for trunk/core muscle preservation, but this effect was minimal. This study utilized caloric restriction and dietary modifications to achieve WL; however, drugs, such as GLP-1s, are increasingly favored for their convenience. Notably, these medications can result in excessive muscle loss, with reductions up to 30%.39 This highlights the potential importance of incorporating progressive resistance, whether through exercise or weighted vest use, for individuals undergoing medically induced WL. Findings from our study suggest that WL strategies for older adults living with overweight or obesity should emphasize the inclusion of traditional progressive RT to optimize fat loss while preserving muscle mass and improving muscle quality.

ack.title: Acknowledgments
ack.p: The authors extend their sincere gratitude to the INVEST in Bone Health study participants for their participation in the study and use of their data for this analysis. We also warmly thank everyone who contributed to the work presented in this secondary analysis.

sec.title: Supplementary material
sec.p: Supplementary material is available at The Journals of Gerontology, Series A: Biological Sciences and Medical Sciences online.

sec.title: Funding
sec.p: This work was supported by the National Institute on Aging (Grants No. R01 AG059186 [K.M.B.], K25 AG058804 [A.A.W.], T32 AG033534, F31 AG086010 [S.D.L.], and P30 AG021332). Jason Pharmaceuticals, a wholly owned subsidiary of Medifast, Inc. made an in-kind product donation for the meal replacements used in this study. Additionally, the authors gratefully acknowledge use of the services and facilities of the Clinical Research Unit and Translational Imaging Program, funded by the National Center for Advancing Translational Sciences (NCATS) and National Institutes of Health (Grant No. UL1TR001420). The funders had no role in the design and conduct of the study; collection, management, analysis, and interpretation of the data; preparation, review, or approval of the manuscript; and decision to submit the manuscript for publication.

sec.title: Conflicts of interest
sec.p: The authors have no conflicts of interest to declare.

sec.title: Data availability
sec.p: Data described in the present study are available upon reasonable request.

sec.title: Authors contributions
sec.p: S. Delanie Lynch, Kristen M. Beavers, Ashley A. Weaver, Daniel P. Beavers, Ryan Barnard, Leon Lenchik (Concept and design), S. Delanie Lynch, Marjorie Howard, Daniel P. Beavers, Ashley A. Weaver, Kristen M. Beavers (Acquisition, analysis, and interpretation of data), S. Delanie Lynch (Drafting of manuscript), and Marjorie Howard, Daniel P. Beavers, Ashley A. Weaver, Kristen M. Beavers, Leon Lenchik, Ryan Barnard, Peggy M. Cawthon, Jessica Bon (Critical revision). All authors approve the final version of the manuscript.
